# Constructing and Leveraging a Knowledge Graph in Elasticsearch for Search Relevance

## Extracting Entities and Relationships from Search Data

Building a knowledge graph (KG) typically begins with extracting structured entities and relationships from your unstructured content or logs. Key techniques include:

- **Natural Language Processing (NLP) Extraction:** Use Named Entity Recognition (NER) to identify entities (people, organizations, places, etc.) in documents, then apply relationship extraction. This can be rule-based (e.g. regex or dependency patterns) or model-based (information extraction models that output subject–predicate–object triples)[\[1\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=There%20are%20several%20popular%20ways,LLMs). For example, an NLP pipeline might detect entities like “Google” (Org) and “Pinterest” (Org) and a phrase linking them (“founded by”), yielding a triple (Google, founded, Pinterest). Large Language Models (LLMs) can also be prompted to generate such triples from text[\[1\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=There%20are%20several%20popular%20ways,LLMs). These approaches convert raw text into candidate nodes and edges for the graph.

- **Co-occurrence Analysis:** Even without explicit linguistic parsing, you can derive relationships by analyzing term co-occurrence in documents. In Elasticsearch, each document can act as a _“super-edge”_ connecting all the terms it contains[\[2\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=With%20elasticsearch%20we%20just%20index,can%20be%20required%20on%20fields). If two entities/terms frequently appear together in many documents (or in the same field), that suggests a meaningful association. Elasticsearch’s built-in significance algorithms can then filter for statistically significant co-occurrences to avoid trivial pairs[\[3\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=connected%20to%20the%20term%20%E2%80%9Cgraph%E2%80%9D,on%20my%20news%20monitoring%20site). For instance, the Elastic Graph API treats indexed terms as vertices and finds connections by summarizing documents where those terms co-occur, using relevance scoring to separate useful signals from noise[\[4\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=The%20graph%20vertices%20are%20simply,typical%20of%20most%20connected%20data). This highlights genuinely related entities rather than just popular ones. Co-occurrence relationships extracted this way (e.g. “Mozart” co-occurs with “Classical music” in many records) form an implicit knowledge graph derived from your data.

- **Mining Search Logs:** If you have user search logs or click data, these can be a rich source of relationship information. Queries that frequently occur together in sessions or queries that lead users to click the same results can be linked as related concepts. In practice, search engines often mine large query logs to find expansions and synonyms – for example, noticing that users who search for “NYC” often also search for “New York City”[\[5\]](https://medium.com/@kyrillos.eskander/query-expansion-using-knowledge-graphs-6353a8043b5a#:~:text=There%20are%20multiple%20techniques%20and,to%20use%20knowledge%20graphs%20instead). Click-through graphs (queries linked to the documents or results they select) can also be projected into query–query or query–term relationships. In the absence of extensive logs (e.g. in a new or domain-specific application), a curated knowledge graph can substitute by providing known relationships[\[5\]](https://medium.com/@kyrillos.eskander/query-expansion-using-knowledge-graphs-6353a8043b5a#:~:text=There%20are%20multiple%20techniques%20and,to%20use%20knowledge%20graphs%20instead).

Combining these techniques is common. You might run NLP on your indexed documents to extract entity mentions and relations (e.g. person X _works at_ company Y) and simultaneously analyze which terms co-occur in documents or queries. The result is a set of entities (nodes) and their relationships (edges) ready to be ingested into Elasticsearch as a knowledge graph.

## Representing the Knowledge Graph in Elasticsearch

Elasticsearch is document-oriented, so a knowledge graph must be mapped into a document schema. There are two broad approaches to represent a KG in Elasticsearch:

1. **Implicit Graph via Existing Documents:** You may not need a separate index at all – Elasticsearch can treat your regular indexed content as a graph of term connections. In this model, each document’s fields provide the links: any terms appearing in the same document are considered connected. The Elastic Graph features leverage this by using aggregations to discover significant term associations on the fly[\[6\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=The%20graph%20API%20provides%20an,related%20terms%20in%20the%20index)[\[7\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=The%20graph%20api%20is%20essentially,on%20my%20news%20monitoring%20site). In graph theory terms, documents act as hyper-edges connecting all their constituent terms. You don’t have to define an explicit ontology or re-index data; instead, you configure the Graph API to focus on certain fields (for example, treat terms in the “keywords” or “tags” field as graph vertices). The Graph explore API (POST index/\_graph/explore) then returns vertices (terms) and connections based on co-occurrence in documents, complete with significance scores and supporting document counts[\[8\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=,1%20%7D). This implicit graph approach is easy to set up – it works out-of-the-box with your existing index and scales using Elasticsearch’s distributed aggregations. However, because it connects terms that co-occur anywhere in a document, it may produce some spurious connections if documents are very large or heterogeneous, so filtering and significance tuning are important (more on that in the Tools section).

2. **Explicit Graph Index (Triples or Adjacency Lists):** In many cases, you’ll want to create a dedicated index to store the knowledge graph in a more structured form (especially if you extracted specific entity relationships via NLP). A common pattern is to index each relationship as a separate document – for example, a JSON document with fields like { "source": "Nancy Pelosi", "relation": "studied_at", "target": "Trinity College", "context": "…sentence or doc excerpt…" }[\[9\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=The%20key%20idea%2C%20then%2C%20is,document%20that%20illustrates%20the%20relation). Each such document represents a triple (entity1, relation, entity2), optionally with extra metadata (context sentence, confidence score, etc.). By indexing triples, you create a corpus of relationship facts that can be queried and filtered using Elasticsearch’s standard query DSL. For instance, you could search this index for all triples where "source": "Nancy Pelosi" to get facts about her, or filter where source or target matches a query entity to retrieve its connections. This is exactly the approach taken in a recent Elasticsearch Labs experiment, which stored millions of extracted triplets in an index and was still able to query subgraphs efficiently[\[10\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=III,to)[\[9\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=The%20key%20idea%2C%20then%2C%20is,document%20that%20illustrates%20the%20relation). Another explicit representation strategy is to maintain separate indices for **nodes** and **edges**[\[11\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=Schema%20of%20data%20indexed%20in,Elasticsearch). A “nodes” index would have one document per entity (with fields for the entity name, type, description, etc.), and an “edges” index would have documents linking two entity IDs (and perhaps a relationship type). The edges index could use a simple schema like { "input": "entityA", "output": "entityB", "relation": "relatedTo", ... } as shown in one example[\[12\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=POST%20_bulk%20%7B%20,03T12%3A10%3A30Z). This directed edge schema can also be used to store graphs derived from other data (e.g. user \-\> item interactions for recommendations). The advantage of separate node and edge indices is that you can lookup node details easily and enforce consistency (each edge refers to existing nodes). The disadvantage is the extra join logic you might need in application code to combine them (Elasticsearch itself doesn’t do graph traversals across indices natively). In practice, if using an explicit triple store in Elasticsearch, many implementations simply denormalize basic node info into each triple document for simplicity (e.g. store the entity names and maybe types in the triple doc, rather than numeric IDs only).

![][image1]  
_Example: Kibana’s Graph UI visualizing term connections from an Elasticsearch index._ Here, each node (green or red circle) represents a term or entity, and edges indicate that two terms appear together in the data (thicker lines often mean a stronger or more significant connection). Under the hood, this was generated by the Graph API aggregations, not a separate graph database[\[4\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=The%20graph%20vertices%20are%20simply,typical%20of%20most%20connected%20data). Such visual exploration helps validate the knowledge graph structure and uncover patterns (e.g. clusters of related technologies in this dataset).

Whether implicit or explicit, the key for representing a KG in Elasticsearch is deciding how you will query it. If you plan to use Elasticsearch’s Graph Explore API or significant terms aggregations, you might rely on the implicit approach (no new index). If you need fine-grained control (e.g. querying specific relationships or multi-hop paths), an explicit index of triples may be more suitable. In fact, you can mix approaches: use NLP to extract high-quality triples and index them, and also leverage co-occurrence on raw text to discover additional connections. The **Graph API** can even work on a triples index by treating the source and target fields as vertices – for example, exploring an index of network connections where documents have input and output fields[\[13\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=,%5B)[\[14\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=,1). In summary, Elasticsearch doesn’t store graphs natively as nodes/edges like a graph DB would, but by structuring data into documents (triples or term lists) you can effectively represent and query a knowledge graph using search queries.

## Indexing and Querying the Graph to Enhance Search Relevance

Once the knowledge graph is in place (whether inferred from your docs or in a dedicated index), you can use it to **augment search queries and results** in several powerful ways. The knowledge graph becomes a source of context that can be consulted at query time to improve relevance. Key applications include:

- **Entity Disambiguation:** A knowledge graph can help interpret user queries by linking ambiguous terms to the correct entities. For example, a query for “Jaguar speed” could refer to the animal or the car model. By recognizing “Jaguar” as an entity node that has multiple types (animal vs. automobile) in the KG, the search system can either prompt the user or use context to disambiguate. Popularity or semantic relatedness in the graph can guide the choice: e.g. if “Jaguar” (car) is a much more common entity in the data or appears frequently with “speed” in connected facts, that might be the intended meaning. In one approach, the query is **parsed and tagged with KG entities** – for instance, identifying that “SoulCycle” in a query maps to either the company or a radio station. The KG or an external lookup (like DBpedia) can provide candidates and popularity scores (reference counts) for each[\[15\]](https://medium.com/@kyrillos.eskander/query-expansion-using-knowledge-graphs-6353a8043b5a#:~:text=As%20shown%20in%20the%20above,In). Using those signals, the system can choose the likely entity (SoulCycle the company, in this example)[\[16\]](https://medium.com/@kyrillos.eskander/query-expansion-using-knowledge-graphs-6353a8043b5a#:~:text=user%20or%20from%20the%20preprocessing,In). This disambiguation step ensures that subsequent search operations target the right entity. Concretely, you might filter or boost results that are annotated with the chosen entity ID, rather than relying on the raw keyword which could hit irrelevant homonyms.

- **Query Expansion with Related Terms:** Query expansion is a classic way to improve recall, and a knowledge graph provides a dynamic source of synonyms and related concepts for expansion. The idea is to broaden the user’s query by adding terms that the KG deems relevant – for example, adding an official name, acronym, or a closely connected entity. This can catch documents that use different terminology or cover related aspects of a topic. In Elasticsearch, you could implement this by programmatically querying the KG index or Graph API for a given query term’s neighbors and then injecting those into a bool query (or using them to build a synonym list at index time). For instance, if a user searches for “COVID”, the system might expand it to also search for “SARS-CoV-2” and “Coronavirus” if the knowledge graph links those as equivalent or related. This process can be as simple as OR-ing in the alternate terms, or more sophisticated (weighted boosts for different relation types). Studies have shown that adding semantically related terms (from thesauri or knowledge bases) to queries often improves recall[\[5\]](https://medium.com/@kyrillos.eskander/query-expansion-using-knowledge-graphs-6353a8043b5a#:~:text=There%20are%20multiple%20techniques%20and,to%20use%20knowledge%20graphs%20instead). In practice, one might restrict expansions to certain relations (e.g. synonym, alias, “is a type of”) to avoid query drift. Elasticsearch’s Graph API itself can suggest strongly connected terms for a given input term – essentially performing an expansion by mining co-occurrence patterns[\[4\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=The%20graph%20vertices%20are%20simply,typical%20of%20most%20connected%20data). For example, if many documents link **“Elasticsearch”** and **“Kibana”**, a search for “Elasticsearch” could automatically also look for “Kibana” due to that graph connection. Graph-based expansion was described in one resource as parsing the query to KG concepts and then **including the closest nodes in the graph** as additional query terms[\[17\]](https://applyingml.com/resources/search-query-matching/#:~:text=probably%20Google%E2%80%99s%2C%20introduced%20almost%2010,edges%20to%20connect%20them%20together). The result is that even if the user’s query is short or not perfectly phrased, the search can find relevant documents that match any of the expanded terms, thus improving recall.

- **Reranking Using Graph Features:** The knowledge graph can also influence the ranking of results after the initial retrieval. One approach is to boost documents that contain entities closely related to the query’s entities. For example, if the query is about **“machine learning”**, a document that mentions **“neural networks”** might be ranked higher if the KG knows neural networks are a subtopic of machine learning. This can be done by enriching documents with entity tags and then using a script score or function score in Elasticsearch that gives a bonus when there’s a KG connection (like a direct edge or a short path) between a query entity and a document entity. Another reranking strategy is to measure the semantic distance between the query and document via the graph: e.g., if the document contains “Google” and the query is “Larry Page”, the KG path Larry Page → founded → Google indicates a strong connection, so that document should rank higher for the query “Larry Page” than one that merely mentions the name without context. Essentially, the graph provides features such as _“has direct entity match”_, _“has 1-hop related entity match”_, _“shares X neighbors with the query entity”_, etc., which can feed a learning-to-rank model. This helps improve **precision** by ensuring that a document isn’t just superficially matching the query terms, but is topically relevant via the entities and relationships it contains. For implementation, you might pre-compute some of these features (e.g., store a field in the document for important related entities) or compute on the fly with additional queries. An example of on-the-fly use is issuing a Graph API query on the query’s entity to get its neighbors, then constructing an Elasticsearch query that boosts any document containing those neighbors. Reranking with a KG is particularly useful for long-tail or complex queries – it helps the search engine “connect the dots” between query and document when the connection isn’t a literal keyword match but a factual relationship.

- **Context-Aware Retrieval:** In more advanced search applications (like conversational search or personalized search), a knowledge graph enables context to be brought into the retrieval process. For instance, consider a user asks, “How tall is he?” right after asking “Who is the CEO of Apple?”. The pronoun “he” is ambiguous on its own, but the context (CEO of Apple) can be resolved via the knowledge graph: **Tim Cook** is CEO of Apple, and Tim Cook’s node in the KG has the property _height_. A context-aware search system could detect that “he” refers to Tim Cook and then reformulate the query to “Tim Cook height”. Similarly, in e-commerce or enterprise search, if you know the user’s current context or profile (say, the user is on a page about **databases**), a query for “security” might be interpreted as “database security” – the KG can link those concepts and the search can prioritize results at their intersection. Contextual query understanding often uses the KG to constrain or expand queries based on what’s currently relevant to the user. In Elasticsearch, this could be implemented by maintaining a context of recent entities (e.g. in a session state) and automatically adding filters or boosts for those. Another form of context is **user intent or domain context**: if your KG classifies content by domain or topic, and you know a user’s domain (say medical vs. legal), the same query term could retrieve domain-specific results by favoring edges in that part of the graph. Overall, the KG acts as a knowledge base that the search engine consults to interpret queries in light of context that isn’t explicitly stated but can be inferred.

By integrating these techniques, Elasticsearch search can go beyond simple keyword matching into **semantic search**. The search workflow might look like: detect entities in query → use KG to get expansions or disambiguation → search the content index (possibly enriched with entity tags) → use KG again to rerank or augment results (like showing a knowledge panel for an entity, or suggesting a follow-up query for a related entity). Each step leverages the KG to improve either recall (finding more relevant docs) or precision (ensuring those docs truly address the user’s intent).

## Improving Recall and Precision with Graph-Powered Search

A well-constructed knowledge graph can dramatically improve search recall (finding all relevant information) without sacrificing precision. It does so by making latent connections explicit. Some notable ways this manifests include:

- **Graph-Augmented RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation):** If you are using Elasticsearch in an AI question-answering setting, incorporating a knowledge graph can solve the “scatterd info” problem. Traditional document RAG often fails when an answer is spread across multiple documents[\[18\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=1,was%20founded%20by%20Ben%20Silbermann%E2%80%A6%E2%80%9D). A knowledge graph bridges those gaps by explicitly linking entities across documents. For example, consider the query: “List some startups that were founded by former employees of Google.” No single document may contain both pieces (“person X worked at Google” and “the same person X founded startup Y”). Classic search might return only documents where “Google” and “founded” co-occur obviously[\[18\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=1,was%20founded%20by%20Ben%20Silbermann%E2%80%A6%E2%80%9D). A knowledge graph, however, could have triplets like (Person X, employer, Google) and (Person X, founded, Startup Y), enabling the system to find **Person X** as the connection and then retrieve the relevant facts. This **greatly improves recall**, because the search is not limited by document boundaries[\[19\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=Graph,not%20limited%20to%20document%20boundaries). In a recent Elastic Labs experiment, the team dynamically constructed a query-specific subgraph and found it allowed an LLM to answer questions that standard RAG missed[\[20\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=individual%20documents%2C%20Graph%20RAG%20can,not%20limited%20to%20document%20boundaries)[\[21\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=,Nancy%20Pelosi%2C%20born_in%2C%20Baltimore). Moreover, the answers assembled from KG triples were more precise and diverse: when asked about a person (e.g. “Tell me all you know about Nancy Pelosi”), a graph-based approach could return distinct facts (education, birthplace, role…) instead of many documents repeating the same fact[\[22\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=For%20instance%20imagine%20asking%2C%20%E2%80%9CTell,and%20structured%20information%2C%20such%20as)[\[21\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=,Nancy%20Pelosi%2C%20born_in%2C%20Baltimore). The graph structure filters out noise and redundancy, focusing on facts, which boosts precision of the final result. In summary, by using a KG within a RAG pipeline, you retrieve a **broader set of facts (higher recall)** and feed the model a consolidated, clean knowledge snippet (improving precision of the answer).

- **Related-Entity Suggestions and Recommendations:** Knowledge graphs also shine at **surfacing related entities** that a user might be interested in, which can be presented as search refinements or recommendations. For instance, if a user searches for a particular product or topic, the system might show a “Related searches” or “Users also looked for” panel powered by the graph. In Kibana’s Graph, this kind of exploration is the primary use-case – you start with one vertex and discover others connected to it. For example, from an IP address node you might discover related domain nodes (common access logs), or from a movie title node you find related actor nodes, etc. These suggestions improve recall by pulling the user’s attention to other relevant concepts they didn’t explicitly search for. In e-commerce, if someone searches for gardening gloves, a graph of co-purchased items could suggest related tools or fertilizers (next-best product recommendations). Elastic’s Graph is explicitly mentioned as useful for these scenarios: _“graph exploration could help you uncover… you might provide graph-based personalized recommendations to your customers”_[\[23\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=,related%20tech). By leveraging connections like “people who bought X also bought Y” or “this paper is frequently cited with that paper” (depending on your data domain), the search interface can guide users to relevant items, increasing the chance they find what they need. These related-entity links come directly from the KG relationships. Since they are based on actual data linkages (not just static synonyms), they tend to maintain precision – e.g. recommending Mozart’s contemporaries to someone who searched Mozart is likely relevant. The knowledge graph can also be used to cluster or group results by entity (improving result organization). For example, a search for “mercury” could yield groups for Mercury the planet, Mercury the element, Mercury the mythological figure, etc., with the KG providing the semantic grouping.

- **Multi-Hop (Path-Based) Query Results:** Perhaps the most unique power of a graph is answering questions about how two things are connected. In an enterprise search or intelligence analysis scenario, a user might query something like “How is Person A connected to Company B?” A direct text search might not find a document containing both A and B, but a KG can reveal the chain of relationships (A → X → Y → B). Elasticsearch isn’t a graph database, but you _can_ perform limited graph traversal by iterative queries on a triples index. One successful strategy is to do a breadth-first search through the KG using Elasticsearch terms queries and filters[\[24\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=To%20check%20if%20two%20entities,are%20connected). For example, first query the KG index for all neighbors of Person A (all triples where A is source or target) and all neighbors of Company B. If there’s an intersection, you found a 2-hop path. If not, take those neighbors (up to some limit) and query again for their neighbors, and so on, until a connection emerges or a hop limit is reached[\[24\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=To%20check%20if%20two%20entities,are%20connected). This is exactly what the Elastic graph RAG demo did to connect Nancy Pelosi to Rachida Dati: it expanded outwards from both entities and checked for a meeting point, up to three hops out[\[24\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=To%20check%20if%20two%20entities,are%20connected)[\[25\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=relation%20store%20contains%20at%20least,hops%20are%20only%20loosely%20related). The figure below illustrates this idea of exploring neighbors of two nodes until a link is found (the “?” indicates an unknown connection that the algorithm is trying to fill in). **Path-finding** via the KG can dramatically improve recall for complex queries – you can retrieve relevant intermediate entities or documents that would be missed by one-shot text search. At the same time, because you’re following actual relationships, the results remain precise (you’re not randomly associating A and B, but finding a concrete chain of evidence connecting them).

![][image2]  
_Illustration: Dynamically building a connection between two query entities (Nancy Pelosi and Rachida Dati) by exploring their relationship graph._ In this approach, each entity’s immediate neighbors (blue nodes) are retrieved (from an Elasticsearch index of triplets) and checked for overlaps. If no direct link is found, the search extends to neighbors-of-neighbors, up to a limited hop count[\[24\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=To%20check%20if%20two%20entities,are%20connected). This on-the-fly subgraph construction yields the shortest path connecting the entities if one exists, which can then be used to answer the query or provide an explanatory snippet. In practice, a limit on expansions (e.g. 3 hops, limiting each node to 100 neighbors) keeps the query performant[\[26\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=the%20nodes%20connected%20to%20the,hops%20are%20only%20loosely%20related)[\[27\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=collect%20more%20than%20100%20neighbours,000%20%3D%2060%20002%20nodes).

Overall, using a graph boosts recall by **transcending document boundaries** – the search isn’t confined to what’s written in one document, but can assemble facts across the corpus. It also can boost precision by focusing on actual known relationships rather than arbitrary keyword coincidences. The Elastic Graph API, for example, by default only surfaces connections that appear in at least 3 documents and that are statistically significant[\[28\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=,relevant%20documents%20for%20a%20query)[\[29\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=%282%29%20set%20the%20,correlation%20with%20the%20sample%3B%20and) (this avoids noise like two rare terms that co-occur once by chance). You can tune this threshold (even down to 1 for exhaustive link discovery), but the point is the graph approach inherently provides a way to filter out less meaningful links, addressing the precision side. When properly constructed, a knowledge graph becomes a **knowledge layer** on top of your index that can be traversed and queried to enrich user searches with understanding and discovery, leading to better search outcomes.

## Tools and Architecture Patterns for Elasticsearch Knowledge Graphs

Elasticsearch provides several native features to help build and use knowledge graphs, and the community has developed patterns to integrate graph technology with the Elastic Stack. Here are some tools and approaches to consider:

- **Elasticsearch Graph API and Kibana Graph:** Elastic has a built-in Graph exploration capability (part of X-Pack features)[\[30\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=is%20not%20that%20recent,quick%20research%20at%20that%20moment). The Graph API (\_graph/explore endpoint) allows you to submit a query and get back vertices and connections as described earlier. You can think of it as a specialized aggregation that finds related terms. The Kibana Graph UI provides an interactive visualization on top of this API[\[31\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=graph,commerce%20customers)[\[32\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=just%20a%20network%20of%20related,related%20terms%20in%20the%20index). It lets you start with a node (or a search query) and interactively expand connected nodes, with controls for significance and depth. This is great for exploratory use cases and prototyping relationships in your data. For example, an analyst could enter a few seed terms (like an IP address or a customer ID) and see a network of linked entities (other IPs, email addresses, etc. that co-occur with the seed in logs) to detect patterns. Under the hood, Graph uses Elasticsearch’s aggregation framework, so it scales with your cluster and can summarize millions of documents into a graph in seconds[\[33\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=be%20interested%20in%3F%20,related%20tech). By default, it uses a sample of documents and significant terms to avoid overwhelming the user with every trivial link[\[28\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=,relevant%20documents%20for%20a%20query). You can configure parameters like sample*size (how many docs per shard to consider), min_doc_count (how many docs must an edge be in to be shown), and use_significance (true/false to use statistical significance)[\[28\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=,relevant%20documents%20for%20a%20query)[\[34\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=%281%29%20increase%20,more%20data%20on%20each%20shard). For example, to get \_all* connections exhaustively, you might set use_significance:false and min_doc_count:1[\[34\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=%281%29%20increase%20,more%20data%20on%20each%20shard), at the cost of more noise. The Graph API is a convenient way to integrate graph queries into your application without standing up separate infrastructure – you can call it from your backend to get related terms for an autocomplete suggestion, for instance. Keep in mind that Graph API requires an appropriate license (it’s available on Elastic Cloud and with commercial subscriptions; a trial can be activated for development)[\[35\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=I%20see%20that%20the%20ELK,quick%20research%20at%20that%20moment).

- **Standard Aggregations for Graph Queries:** If you prefer not to rely on the licensed Graph API, you can still perform graph-like queries using Elasticsearch’s standard aggregations in creative ways. A common pattern is to use a **terms aggregation** to get the neighbors of a given node. For example, to find what “object1” connects to, you can run a terms agg on the output field with a filter input: "object1"[\[36\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=GET%20test%2F_search%20%7B%20,) – the buckets returned are effectively the nodes linked to “object1”, with doc_count as the edge weight (frequency)[\[37\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=Say%20we%20want%20to%20answer,in%20decreasing%20number%20of%20%E2%80%9Cdoc_count%E2%80%9D)[\[38\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=,1000). To find connections between two sets of nodes, the **adjacency_matrix aggregation** is useful. This agg lets you specify multiple filters and returns a matrix of how many documents match each filter combination. You could set up filters like “documents where input=A” and “documents where input=B” and one for “input=A OR B”, etc., to see if A and B ever occur together (in the same document)[\[7\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=The%20graph%20api%20is%20essentially,on%20my%20news%20monitoring%20site). Another approach is sequential querying: first get neighbors of A, then check which of those appear as neighbors of B (an application-side join of two terms aggs). This is essentially what one would code to do multi-hop traversal if not using the Graph API. Some community articles (such as a 2024 Medium series on querying Elasticsearch data as a graph) outline how to construct these queries step by step and even how to format the results for visualization[\[39\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=Proposed%20architecture)[\[40\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=ES%20graph%20explore%20API). The takeaway is that **Elasticsearch’s query DSL is flexible enough** to retrieve graph structures – the developer may just need to orchestrate multiple queries and combine results. For instance, a script could automatically expand nodes hop by hop using the results of the previous search (similar to how you’d do it in SQL or code). This approach can be sufficient for relatively short traversals or for building small subgraphs on demand, especially if the underlying data is indexed for quick term lookups (using keyword fields for entities is important here for exact matching).

- **Data Modeling and Indexing Pipelines:** In setting up a graph in Elasticsearch, you should consider how to model your entities and relationships for efficient access. Using **keyword (untokenized) fields** for entity identifiers or names is crucial so that aggregations and term queries treat each entity as a single unit (as opposed to full-text analysis which would split them). If your data entities have multiple name variants or aliases, you might index them as multi-fields or in a separate alias mapping index. Elasticsearch’s **enrich processor** can assist during indexing: for example, as you ingest documents, you could use an enrich policy to look up each recognized entity in a knowledge index and add a canonical ID or type to the document. This way, your main index gets tagged with KG entity IDs, enabling precise lookup and disambiguation at search time (you can filter results by an entity ID or join across indices by searching the KG index for an ID and then the content index for that ID). The enrich processor essentially performs a key/value lookup from one index to add fields to documents in another index at ingest[\[41\]](http://pipiho.com/es/7.7/en/ingest-enriching-data.html#:~:text=Enrich%20your%20data%20,use%20the%20enrich%20processor%20to). This is useful if you want to avoid query-time joins – you pre-compute the linkage of content to the KG. For example, if a document mentions “IBM”, an enrich processor could add "entity_id": 1234 (the ID for IBM in the KG) to the doc. Then a user query for “IBM” could directly match on entity_id:1234 rather than text, achieving high precision (no false hits on “IBM” as an acronym for other things). Designing such pipelines requires identifying entities (via NLP or other means) during ingest, which can be done with tools like the Ingest NLP plugin or external processing before indexing.

- **Integration with Graph Databases:** Sometimes the question arises: should you use Elasticsearch or a graph database (like Neo4j)? The good news is these are not mutually exclusive – they can complement each other. Elasticsearch is not optimized for deep graph traversals (due to its sharded, document-centric architecture)[\[42\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=Elasticsearch%20is%20not%20optimised%20for,connected%20to%20a%20%E2%80%9Cthread%E2%80%9D%20object), especially if you need to do complex queries like variable-length paths, cycles, graph algorithms, etc. Neo4j or similar graph DBs excel at that with index-free adjacency and graph query languages (Cypher). However, graph databases are not as good for full-text search or analytics across many nodes. A common architecture is to use a **hybrid approach**: maintain the authoritative knowledge graph in a graph database, and push selective “views” or indexes of that data into Elasticsearch for text querying and integration with other data. For example, GraphAware (a Neo4j partner) provides a Neo4j-to-Elasticsearch connector that automatically indexes graph data into Elasticsearch documents[\[43\]](https://graphaware.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/graphpoweredsearch-neo4j-elasticsearch.pdf#:~:text=At%20this%20point%2C%20data%20is,the%20product%20catalog%2C%20since%20it)[\[44\]](https://graphaware.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/graphpoweredsearch-neo4j-elasticsearch.pdf#:~:text=documents,viable%20tool%20in%20a%20relevant). In such an architecture, your application can query Neo4j for pure graph questions (like “find shortest path between X and Y”) and use Elasticsearch to perform keyword searches or to filter graph data by text criteria. The front-end might first call Elasticsearch to get a list of relevant entities/docs, then fetch their relationships from Neo4j for display. Or vice versa: find a set of entities via a graph query, then use their IDs to fetch documents from ES. The decision of using Elasticsearch alone vs. a graph DB depends on your use case complexity and scale. Elastic’s Mark Harwood (author of the Graph feature) summarized it well: with Elasticsearch, there’s no need to decompose your data into a pure node-edge model upfront – you can index whole documents and still discover connections[\[45\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=documents%20,connected%20to%20a%20%E2%80%9Cthread%E2%80%9D%20object) – but you operate within constraints (limited traversal depth, need for significance filtering). With a dedicated graph store, you model the graph explicitly and can traverse arbitrarily, but you then need to integrate that with search separately[\[42\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=Elasticsearch%20is%20not%20optimised%20for,connected%20to%20a%20%E2%80%9Cthread%E2%80%9D%20object)[\[2\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=With%20elasticsearch%20we%20just%20index,can%20be%20required%20on%20fields). For many “knowledge graph search” scenarios (FAQ bots, simple recommendation, entity tagging), Elasticsearch alone is sufficient and simpler. For highly interconnected data with complex graph patterns (fraud detection with elaborate networks, knowledge graphs with millions of nodes where you run graph algorithms), a dual setup might be warranted.

- **Community Solutions and Examples:** Beyond Elastic’s official offerings, the community has shared numerous examples and libraries for working with knowledge graphs on Elasticsearch. There are Medium articles and Github projects demonstrating end-to-end pipelines: extracting entities with spaCy or HuggingFace models, indexing triples into Elasticsearch, and building a UI to query the graph. Some notable patterns include using **Kibana Canvas or custom d3.js visualizations** to display subgraphs retrieved from Elasticsearch (for those who don’t have Kibana Graph enabled). Others have used **OpenRefine or RDF libraries** to transform data into triples before indexing. If your knowledge graph data is already in RDF format (with URIs, etc.), you can index those triples in Elasticsearch and even use the **SQL or Painless scripting** to query them in a SPARQL-like fashion (though not as flexibly as a true triplestore). Another emerging area is using **vector embeddings alongside the graph** – e.g. store vector representations of entities in Elasticsearch’s vector fields for semantic similarity search, complementing the explicit graph links. This can help find related entities that aren’t directly connected by a known edge but are conceptually similar. Finally, Elastic’s recent work on **Graph RAG** (as discussed) is available as a detailed example from Elastic Labs[\[46\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=Retrieval,for%20deeper%2C%20more%20contextual%20retrieval)[\[47\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=To%20overcome%20this%20limitation%2C%20researchers,based%20implementations), which can serve as a reference architecture: it shows how to store a large triplet set in Elasticsearch and perform on-the-fly subgraph extraction with speed. They managed sub-second graph queries over a KG with \~2 million relations by leveraging filtered index searches instead of graph DB traversals[\[48\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=This%20strategy%20allows%20us%20to,And%20finally%20connect%20both%20politicians)[\[49\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=B,Elasticsearch%E2%80%99s%20plasticity). This demonstrates that with the right indexing and query strategy, Elasticsearch can handle substantial knowledge graphs within a search context.

In conclusion, constructing a knowledge graph for Elasticsearch involves extracting structured knowledge from your data, choosing a representation (implicit vs explicit index), and then using Elasticsearch’s capabilities to query that knowledge in tandem with traditional search. By using tools like the Graph API, significant terms aggregations, and carefully designed indices, you can achieve powerful search enhancements: disambiguating user intent, expanding queries with domain knowledge, recommending relevant content, and answering relational questions – all on the Elasticsearch platform. The result is a search experience that is significantly more intelligent, context-aware, and user-friendly, powered by the connections within your data.

**Sources:** The information above incorporates concepts and examples from Elastic’s official documentation and labs (for Graph and RAG)[\[6\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=The%20graph%20API%20provides%20an,related%20terms%20in%20the%20index)[\[1\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=There%20are%20several%20popular%20ways,LLMs), community articles[\[37\]](https://medium.com/@lewis.won/how-to-query-structured-data-as-graph-from-elasticsearch-b948ec5ba34f#:~:text=Say%20we%20want%20to%20answer,in%20decreasing%20number%20of%20%E2%80%9Cdoc_count%E2%80%9D)[\[5\]](https://medium.com/@kyrillos.eskander/query-expansion-using-knowledge-graphs-6353a8043b5a#:~:text=There%20are%20multiple%20techniques%20and,to%20use%20knowledge%20graphs%20instead), and expert discussions on Elastic forums[\[2\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=With%20elasticsearch%20we%20just%20index,can%20be%20required%20on%20fields)[\[7\]](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/knowlegde-graphs-elk-and-neo4j/351686#:~:text=The%20graph%20api%20is%20essentially,on%20my%20news%20monitoring%20site). These sources illustrate how entity extraction, co-occurrence analysis, and graph indexing can be implemented in Elasticsearch, and how features like query expansion and graph traversal improve search relevance. The Kibana Graph image and examples[\[4\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=The%20graph%20vertices%20are%20simply,typical%20of%20most%20connected%20data)[\[23\]](https://www.elastic.co/docs/explore-analyze/visualize/graph#:~:text=,related%20tech) show practical applications like recommendations and fraud analysis. The strategies described – from using \_graph/explore to custom aggregations – are grounded in real implementations as referenced throughout the answer.

---

[\[26\]](https://www.elastic.co/search-labs/blog/rag-graph-traversal#:~:text=the%20nodes%20connected%20to%20the,hops%20are%20only%20loosely%20related) \- Elasticsearch Labs
